heading: NOTE AL PROEMIO GENERALE
card_summary: This chunk consists of Saverio Campanini's critical apparatus for Zorzi's General Proem, providing 32 numbered source citations that identify the authorities underpinning Zorzi's opening arguments. Key sources annotated include Proclus's Commentary on the Timaeus, Eusebius of Caesarea, Diogenes Laertius, Plato's Timaeus and Epinomis, Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite, Philostratus's Life of Apollonius, the Pythagorean philosopher Hippasus of Sybaris (transmitted via Clement of Alexandria and Iamblichus), Hierocles, Plotinus, Themistius, Boethius, and Dion Chrysostom. Biblical citations are identified from 1 Corinthians 1:23, Colossians 2:8, Psalm 119:11 (Vulgate), Isaiah 6:1 and 6:3 and 66:1, Ezekiel 10:10, John 1:3-4, Exodus 34:33, and 1 Samuel 16:16-23. Campanini flags that note 16 traces Zorzi's citation of an Arabic maxim attributed to Avenzoar back through Pico della Mirandola's Oratio (par. 36) and the astronomer Abumasar, and that note 23 corrects an erroneous Hebrew citation of Ezekiel 10:10 present in both the first and second editions of De Harmonia Mundi.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 140 of the Campanini edition of Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* (Bompiani, 2010) presents the full critical apparatus — thirty-two numbered endnotes — for the *Proemio Generale* (General Proem). This section is purely editorial, the work of Saverio Campanini rather than Zorzi himself, and its function is to identify, locate, and sometimes correct the sources that Zorzi wove into his proem without explicit citation. Read together, these notes constitute a map of the intellectual inheritance of Venetian Christian Kabbalah circa 1525.

## Neoplatonic and Philosophical Sources

The notes reveal that the proem draws heavily on the Neoplatonic tradition, especially Proclus. Notes 1, 10, and 13 cite the *Commentarium in Timaeum* (1,358; 1,302; 3,26 respectively), confirming that Proclus's exegesis of Plato's *Timaeus* is a structural scaffold for Zorzi's cosmological harmonics. Note 9 adds Plotinus (*Enneads* 5,8,10) on the intelligible beauty of the cosmos, while note 14 supplies Themistius's *Paraphrase of Aristotle's De Anima* (5,3,11–12), documenting Zorzi's eclectic synthesis of Platonic, Plotinian, and Peripatetic psychology.

Plato himself is cited at notes 3 and 17: the *Timaeus* (72d) on prophetic inspiration, and the *Epinomis* (977e–978a) on number and astronomy as paths to wisdom.

Note 5 identifies a passage from Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite (*De divinis nominibus* 1,1–2), crucial to Zorzi's hierarchical theology of divine names — a cornerstone of the Christian Kabbalist program of reconciling Jewish divine nomenclature with Christian Trinitarian doctrine.

Note 6 cites Philostratus's *Life of Apollonius of Tyana* (2,26), illustrating Zorzi's willingness to draw on pagan theurgic biography alongside scriptural and Neoplatonic authorities.

## The Pythagorean Tradition

Notes 7 and 8 address Pythagorean sources. Note 7 is particularly valuable as a piece of textual criticism: Zorzi refers to the Pythagorean philosopher whom he names "Hippasus," but Campanini clarifies that this is in fact **Hippasus of Sybaris** (6th–5th century BCE). The corrupted form of the name, as Campanini notes, already appears in Clement of Alexandria (*Stromata* V, 58), but the immediate source Zorzi most likely used was **Iamblichus**, *De vita Pythagorica* (17,75).

> *La forma scorretta si trova già in Clemente Alessandrino (Stromata V, 58), ma la fonte da cui Zorzi ricava la notizia è probabilmente Giamblico, De vita Pyth. 17,75.*
> <cite>Campanini, note 7, p. 2755</cite>

This is characteristic of Renaissance transmission: errors of attribution pass through patristic intermediaries and reach humanist compilers already garbled. Note 8 supplies Hierocles's *Commentary on the Golden Verses of Pythagoras* (preface), reinforcing the Pythagorean-harmonic dimension of the proem.

Note 15 cites Boethius's *De institutione arithmetica* (1,2), the standard medieval entry point into Pythagorean number theory, demonstrating how Zorzi moves from ancient Greek sources through their late-antique Latin transmitters.

## The Arabic and Pico della Mirandola Transmission

Note 16 is among the most editorially significant entries in this chunk. Zorzi cites a maxim attributed to "Avenzoar" (the Andalusian physician Ibn Zuhr), but Campanini cannot locate a precise textual match and concludes that Zorzi's direct source is **Pico della Mirandola's *Oratio de hominis dignitate*** (par. 36). Pico in turn attributes the saying to "Avenzoar" as reported by the Arab astronomer **Abumasar** (Abu Ma'shar al-Balkhi).

> *La fonte immediata di questa citazione (che manca, se non sbaglio, di un riscontro testuale preciso) è Pico della Mirandola, Oratio, par. 36. A sua volta Pico afferma che la massima di "Avenzoar" è riferita dall'astronomo arabo Abumasar.*
> <cite>Campanini, note 16, p. 2755</cite>

This note crystallizes the layered transmission characteristic of Zorzi's method: an Arabic medical-philosophical maxim, filtered through an Arab astronomer, cited by the arch-synthesizer Pico, and then absorbed into Zorzi's Venetian Franciscan harmony. The chain Abumasar → Ibn Zuhr → Pico → Zorzi exemplifies how the *prisca sapientia* tradition operated as a cumulative and often unverifiable inheritance.

## Biblical Citations and Their Liturgical-Prophetic Weight

The notes identify a dense cluster of scriptural citations that anchor the proem's theological claims:

- **1 Corinthians 1:23** (note 11): Paul's "scandal of the cross" to Jews and foolishness to Greeks — a key proof-text for Zorzi's argument that divine wisdom exceeds Greek philosophical categories.
- **Colossians 2:8** (note 12): the warning against "philosophy and empty deceit" — rhetorically deployed to inoculate the reader against the charge that Zorzi's Platonism is heterodox.
- **Psalm 119:11 (Vulgate)** (note 18): the hiding of divine words in the heart.
- **Isaiah 6:1 and 6:3** (notes 19, 21): the vision of the Lord enthroned and the *Trisagion* ("Holy, holy, holy") — central to Kabbalistic angelology and the Merkavah tradition.
- **Isaiah 66:1** (note 20): heaven as God's throne and earth as his footstool, relevant to Zorzi's spatial cosmology of divine harmony.
- **Ezekiel 10:10** (note 23): the vision of the four wheels (*ophanim*), a locus classicus of Jewish mystical speculation. Campanini flags a significant textual error: the Hebrew citation of this verse is reproduced incorrectly in both the **first and second editions** of *De Harmonia Mundi*.

> *La citazione ebraica nella prima e nella seconda edizione è riportata in modo errato.*
> <cite>Campanini, note 23, p. 2756</cite>

This error in the Hebrew is not trivial. Ezekiel 10:10 is a cornerstone of the Merkavah tradition and its visual-numerical symbolism (the four wheels as fourfold cosmic structure). An error here in both early editions suggests either a compositor's mistake or a corrupt exemplar, and Campanini's correction is a substantive contribution to Zorzi studies.

- **John 1:3–4** (note 24): the Prologue's "all things were made through him, and without him nothing was made" — the Logos doctrine that Zorzi consistently aligns with Kabbalistic *Chokhmah*.
- **Exodus 34:33** (note 26): Moses veiling his face — deployed as a figure for the concealment of divine mysteries.
- **1 Samuel 16:16–23** (note 29): David playing the harp before Saul, the scriptural warrant for music's therapeutic and quasi-theurgic power.

## Musical and Rhetorical Sources

Note 27 cites Boethius's *De institutione musica* (1,1), which also transmits a fragment of Cicero's *De consiliis suis* — linking the music-of-the-spheres tradition directly to Roman oratorical culture. Note 28 supplies Dion Chrysostom's *De regno* (1,1), the opening of a text that uses Homeric verse as a vehicle for philosophical kingship — another instance of Zorzi's synthesis of pagan rhetorical with theological harmonic thought.

## Geographical Note

Note 25 is a brief but telling geographical gloss: Campanini clarifies that a reference in the proem is to the toponym *Arabia felix* — the ancient designation for the southwestern Arabian Peninsula, associated in Renaissance thought with spices, incense, and the transmission of wisdom from the East.

## Philological Contribution: Origen

Note 31 cites Origen's *Homiliae in Numeros* (15,2), connecting Zorzi's proem to the allegorical exegetical tradition of the Alexandrian school — relevant to his reading of the Hebrew Bible as a concealed harmonic code.

Note 32 refers to Tosi 1991, n. 773, a standard repertory of classical commonplaces (*Dizionario delle sentenze latine e greche*), suggesting that one of Zorzi's citations is a proverbial maxim traceable in the classical tradition.

## Significance

The apparatus for the *Proemio Generale* reveals the proem as one of the most source-dense passages in *De Harmonia Mundi*. Within 32 notes covering a relatively brief prefatory text, Campanini identifies authorities spanning: Presocratic Pythagoreanism (Hippasus via Iamblichus and Clement); Platonic dialogues (*Timaeus*, *Epinomis*); Neoplatonism (Plotinus, Proclus, Hierocles, Themistius); Christian Neoplatonism (Pseudo-Dionysius, Origen, Boethius); Arabic philosophical transmission (Abumasar, Ibn Zuhr); the Italian Renaissance synthesis (Pico della Mirandola); and the full range of Old and New Testament prophetic and wisdom literature. The correction of the Hebrew citation of Ezekiel 10:10 and the tracing of the Avenzoar maxim through Pico to Abumasar are Campanini's most substantive editorial interventions in this chunk.
